items.q: Is bandwidth the speed at which data travels along the cable?
items.correct: No - bandwidth is the capacity of the channel (bits per second it can carry); the travel delay of a signal is latency, a separate measure.
items.misconception: Yes - higher bandwidth means each bit physically moves faster.
items.random_wrong: Yes - bandwidth is measured in metres per second.
items.why: Students picture a faster car rather than a wider road. Bandwidth is how much fits through per second; latency is how long any one bit takes to arrive.

items.q: Does a device keep the same IP address forever?
items.correct: No - IP addresses are logical and commonly assigned dynamically (e.g. by DHCP), so they can change; the MAC address is the fixed hardware identifier.
items.misconception: Yes - every device's IP address is burned in at manufacture.
items.random_wrong: Yes - changing an IP address is impossible without a new device.
items.why: The roles of the two addresses get swapped. Permanence belongs to the MAC address on the NIC; the IP address depends on which network the device joins.

items.q: Do the packets of a message always arrive in the order they were sent?
items.correct: No - packets may travel different routes and arrive out of order; sequence numbers let the receiving end reassemble them correctly.
items.misconception: Yes - networks guarantee packets arrive in their original order.
items.random_wrong: Yes - packets are chained together so they cannot separate.
items.why: The reassembled message hides the chaos underneath. Independent routing is the whole point of packet switching, and ordering is restored by transport-layer protocols like TCP.

items.q: In the TCP/IP stack, which layer is responsible for routing packets between different networks?
items.why: It's Network (internet) layer. “Application layer” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A protocol is best described as:
items.correct: An agreed set of rules governing communication between devices
items.why: It's An agreed set of rules governing communication between devices. “A type of network cable” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In packet switching, the packets of one message:
items.correct: May take different routes and are reassembled at the destination
items.misconception: Must all follow exactly the same route
items.random_wrong: Are always delivered in the order sent
items.why: Correct answer: May take different routes and are reassembled at the destination. “Must all follow exactly the same route” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which statement about IP and MAC addresses is correct?
items.correct: A MAC address is fixed to the hardware; an IP address is logical and can change
items.misconception: Both are assigned permanently at manufacture
items.random_wrong: An IP address identifies the network card hardware
items.why: It's A MAC address is fixed to the hardware; an IP address is logical and can change. “Both are assigned permanently at manufacture” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many seconds does it take to transfer a 32 megabit file over a connection with a bandwidth of 8 megabits per second?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
